critique.summary: The article describes the author's process of reverse-engineering Viktor, an AI coworker developed by Zeta Labs, by extracting and analyzing its internal files and logs. The author then used this information to recreate Viktor's system architecture and implemented an open-source version called OpenViktor.
mainPoints: Zeta Labs announced Viktor, an AI coworker connecting to over 3,000 tools and quickly gaining popularity.
The author obtained a backup of Viktor's workspace, including SDK code, logs, and skill prompts, revealing detailed internal workings.
Logs showed detailed event tracking and workflow prompts demonstrated sophisticated business logic embedded in Viktor.
Using the gathered data, the author reverse-engineered Viktor's architecture and created detailed documentation and diagrams.
The author successfully implemented an open-source version of Viktor named OpenViktor, available for self-hosting and with a managed instance.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about reverse-engineering Viktor are consistent with the author's detailed descriptions and evidence of accessing internal files and logs. The existence of the open-source project OpenViktor on GitHub supports the claim of reimplementation. However, the article is a personal blog post without independent verification or multiple sources, and it relies heavily on the author's own exploration and interpretation. The claim that the author may have better documentation than Zeta Labs is speculative and unverifiable. The description of Viktor's capabilities and popularity aligns with publicly available information about the product, but some claims such as 1M ARR in 3 hours are cited from Zeta Labs' announcement and not independently confirmed.
considerations: The ethical and legal implications of reverse-engineering proprietary AI software are not discussed.
Potential security or privacy concerns related to the ease of accessing logs and internal data are mentioned but not deeply analyzed.
The article does not provide perspectives from Zeta Labs or other independent experts on the reverse-engineering or the open-source implementation.
The performance, completeness, and fidelity of the OpenViktor implementation compared to the original Viktor are not evaluated.
The article does not address the sustainability or maintenance plans for the open-source project.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the legal and ethical implications of reverse-engineering Viktor, with some commenters emphasizing the ease of replicating such tools and the potential for increased competition. Others focus on the legal boundaries under Dutch law and the nature of the software as mostly orchestration around existing AI models.

comments.author: redfloatplane
comments.text:
There are gonna be some really interesting legal decisions to read in the coming years, that’s for sure…

---

The rest of this comment is irrelevant, but leaving for posterity, I had the wrong Viktor - it's getviktor.com not viktor.ai:

Edit: this one particularly interesting to me as both parties are in the EU. VIKTOR.ai is a Dutch company and the author of this post is Polish.

The ToS for Viktor.ai include the following fun passages:

> 18.1. The Agreement and these Terms & Conditions are governed by Dutch law and the Agreement and these Terms & Conditions will be interpreted in accordance with Dutch law.

18.2. All disputes arising from or arising in connection with the Agreement and/or the Terms & Conditions will be submitted exclusively to the competent court in Rotterdam, The Netherlands.

7.3. The Customer is not permitted to change, remove or make unrecognizable any mark showing VIKTOR's Intellectual Property Rights to the Software. The Customer is not permitted to use or register any trademark or design or any domain name of VIKTOR or a similar name or sign in any country.

8.5. The Customer may not cause or allow any reproduction, imitation, duplication, copying, sale, resale, leasing or trading of the Services and/or the Software, or any part thereof.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and accurate summary of the legal terms governing Viktor.ai, highlighting jurisdiction and intellectual property restrictions, which are crucial for understanding the legal context.

comments.author: yashasolutions
comments.text:
Nice. Probably worth making a local copy before it gets taken down.

(Re: legal - why even bother with a court decision when it’s on GitHub? A takedown is much simpler. We've seen this before, like when Meta went after people reverse-engineering their API)

That said there may not be much here thats actually protectable. It's mostly a CLI orchestrating other tools, and the same functionality could likely be reproduced fairly easily, especially with AI.

Still, props to him for writing a proper blog post and explaining the process
comments.analysis: The comment correctly identifies the technical nature of the project as orchestration rather than proprietary AI, and realistically assesses the likelihood of takedown and ease of replication.

comments.author: kklisura
comments.text: I find it fascinating that this is just some _plumbing_ around some LLM - sold as a product and "AI coworker". They don't own the LLM model. You're just one silent update from not delivering what you promised.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully points out that the product is mainly integration around an existing LLM, which is an important consideration for evaluating its uniqueness and value.

comments.author: hmokiguess
comments.text:
A lot of folks talking about the legality basis of this approach, I think the focus should be more around the true moat behind these hype growth hack companies out there.

This sort of exposure is good, in my opinion it enables competition to emerge with true business moat rather than just "do it first, do it fast, worry last" type of thing we're seeing.
comments.analysis: This comment shifts the discussion from legality to business strategy, suggesting that exposing such projects encourages competition based on real value rather than speed or hype.

comments.author: empath75
comments.text:
I just want to point out _how easy it is_ to build any of these tools yourself.

I got inspired by nano-claw and built on some of it's ideas to build a whole k8s hosted autonomous agent platform and got it into production in 2 weeks. It's just some api calls and container orchestration. The only hard problem _and it is hard_, is securing it, because you basically have to treat the agents as potentially malicious.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a practical perspective on the technical challenges involved, emphasizing security as the main difficulty rather than the core functionality.

comments.author: klustregrif
comments.text: Ignorering the questionable legality of this entire thing, which might fall out in favor of it being a legal activity. Calling the project OpenViktor is as dumb as committing secrets to GitHub. For the love of reason call it something else.
comments.analysis: While the comment correctly notes the potential legal ambiguity, it is somewhat emotional and less constructive, focusing on naming rather than substantive issues.

comments.author: popcorncowboy
comments.text: Every part of this is genuinely funny. Viktor launching basically OpenClaw for slack (because claw is MIT so yolo let's go mac some monnney and PJ outta Dubai etc etc). Some guy getting all their source code just by asking Viktor for it. Then rebuilding their core in a couple days because there's basically nothing there. push MIT to Github. TeamViktor's faces surely? But they're busy diamond handsing all the way to the moon so probably don't even care. XD
comments.analysis: This comment is mostly humorous and sarcastic, providing little substantive analysis and leaning towards trolling.

comments.author: cubefox
comments.analysis: The comment is a brief ethical judgment without explanation or supporting argument, making it unhelpful for deeper understanding.

comments.author: dirk94018
comments.text: "Driven by curiosity and envy, I tested it out." Two days of work driven by envy. Not what open source should be about.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a subjective moral critique of the motivation behind the project, which is not directly relevant to the technical or legal discussion.
